title: Tomato Sauce on Cotton
stainChemistry: Combination stains contain protein, fat, and tannin components. Each component requires different chemistry: protein needs detergent, fat needs solvent, tannin needs peroxide, minerals need acid. Standard approach: solvent first (removes oil/fat carrier), then tannin formula (color), then H₂O₂ (residual pigment). Cotton (cellulose fiber) tolerates most agents well.
whyThisWorks: Tomato-based stains contain both plant pigments and oil — the pigment provides the red color while the oil helps it soak in deeper. Cotton absorbs quickly and holds stains deep in the weave. The right cleaning agent breaks the stain's bond to the surface so it can be rinsed away cleanly.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply POG to dissolve the oil portion of the tomato sauce stain. The oil must be removed first so the tannin treatment can reach the color underneath. Blot with clean absorbent cloth.
2. Apply Tannin to address the color component. Tannin in tomato-based stains creates the red/orange discoloration. Tamp gently and work from edge toward center.
3. Apply H₂O₂ 6% to oxidize any remaining tomato sauce pigment. Check progress every few minutes. Test on colored fabrics first.
4. Flush with NSD to remove all spotting agent residue. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
5. Apply diluted acetic acid (1:10) to neutralize any remaining alkaline residue. Blot dry with clean absorbent cloth.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — hot water can set any protein component permanently
warnings: Applying all treatments simultaneously — creates a chemical slurry that's harder to remove
Using hot water early — can set the protein component permanently
Not rinsing completely between treatments — old chemistry interferes with new treatment
Over-agitating the stain — spreads each component deeper into the fiber

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden seam
homeSolutions.instruction: Tomato sauce is a combo stain — lycopene pigment, oil, and sometimes protein from meat or cream. You'll use several cleaners in sequence, so test first. On an inside seam, dab a drop of Dawn solution and a drop of 3% hydrogen peroxide. Blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If no dye transfers and the fabric looks unchanged, proceed. If the seam loses color, go to step 8.

Lift off pooled sauce, meat chunks, or cheese with a dull butter knife or plastic card, scraping away from the center. Most tomato stains have half their bulk sitting on the surface — removing it makes every following step 3× more effective. Don't press, don't wipe in circles, don't let solids sit.

Turn the fabric inside out and run cold water through the BACK of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Push the sauce back out the way it came in. Cold matters: heat sets lycopene (the red pigment) into the fiber permanently, and protein from meat or cream sauces also cross-links with heat. Skip hot water — even if the sauce was hot.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — tackle the oil first
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab a small amount of Dawn directly on the stain (no water yet). Work it in with a fingertip for 30 seconds. Let it sit 5 minutes. Dawn emulsifies the oil carrier in the sauce so the next step can reach the pigment underneath. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Most fresh tomato stains lose most of their intensity in this step alone.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide — tackle the pigment
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one teaspoon of Dawn with two tablespoons of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dab onto the remaining stain. Let it dwell 10 minutes. Peroxide oxidizes lycopene — the specific chromophore that makes tomato red. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Repeat up to two more times if a shadow is still visible.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia or bleach — toxic gas. On brightly colored cotton, test the seam first.

homeSolutions.instruction: If the sauce had meat or cream, add an enzyme step: apply OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout per the label. Dwell 30 minutes (enzymes digest the protein component). Launder on warm (not hot) with your normal detergent. Cotton tolerates warm water once pigment is out — but keep it under 90°F while any shadow remains.

homeSolutions.agent: Air dry + daylight inspect — sunlight finish on white
homeSolutions.instruction: Hang or lay flat. Inspect in daylight, NOT a lamp. If the cotton is white and a faint yellow/orange shadow remains, lay the garment flat in direct sunlight for 1–3 hours. UV naturally bleaches lycopene over time without chemistry. Skip sunlight on colored cotton (fades dye). If shadow persists, repeat steps 5–6 before drying on heat.

If the sauce was set by a dryer, sat for more than 24 hours, or survived two full home rounds, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Tomato sauce on cotton — [plain / meat / cream-based], treated at home with Dawn + 3% peroxide + [enzyme]. No heat applied. Please use sequential POG → tannin formula → peroxide treatment." That specifies the exact order pros need.

steps: 1. Scrape or wipe away any solid material. Use cold water.
2. Mix mild dish soap into cold water. Blot the solution onto the stain.
4. Blot with a clean white cloth. Repeat if needed.
5. Rinse with cold water. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain is still visible after multiple treatments, it may be permanently set. For garments you care about, consider professional cleaning before attempting further home treatment.
warnings: Always blot — never rub. Rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fabric.
Always scrape off solid material first — don't smear it around.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone.

neverDo: Apply all treatments simultaneously — creates a chemical slurry
Use hot water early — can set protein component permanently
Skip rinsing completely between treatment steps
fiberSensitivities: Combination stains on delicate fibers require careful chemistry sequencing. Cotton tolerates most agents. For colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam before use.

escalation.when: Sequential treating fails, or stain components have cross-linked together
escalation.whatToTell: Tomato sauce stain on cotton, combination of oil and tannin pigment. Treated at home with NSD. Age unknown. Please use sequential solvent, tannin formula, and oxidizer treatment.

contextQuestions: Is the stain fresh or dried?
Was this plain tomato sauce or did it contain meat/cream?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Meat-containing sauce adds protein. Cream-based sauces add fat. Dryer exposure bakes pigment into the fiber. On colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam first.
